question: [File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer to set isAuthorized[victim][attacker]=true by acting as an authorized delegate, and then use that authorization to call Midnight.withdraw(market, units, victim, attacker) to drain the victim's credit? Preconditions: attacker is an authorized delegate of victim; victim has credit in a market. Call sequence: (1) attacker signs Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true, nonce=N}; (2) attacker submits via ecrecoverAuthorizer (already authorized, so signer check passes); (3) attacker calls Midnight.withdraw(market, units, victim, attacker); (4) victim's credit drained. Invariant tested: authorization grants via EcrecoverAuthorizer must require the authorizer's own signature. Scoped impact: unauthorized withdrawal of victim's credit. Proof idea: unit test asserting attacker can withdraw victim's credit after self-granting authorization via delegate path.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker front-run a victim's authorization revocation (isAuthorized=false) by submitting a new authorization grant (isAuthorized=true) with the same nonce, preventing the victim from revoking a compromised operator's access? Preconditions: victim wants to revoke operator; attacker has obtained a grant signature for the same nonce. Call sequence: (1) victim signs Authorization{authorized=operator, isAuthorized=false, nonce=N}; (2) attacker has a grant signature for nonce=N (e.g., from a previous signing session); (3) attacker front-runs with grant; (4) victim's revocation reverts InvalidNonce(); (5) operator retains access. Invariant tested: revocation must not be blockable by a front-runner holding a grant signature for the same nonce. Scoped impact: inability to revoke compromised operator, enabling continued unauthorized access. Proof idea: Foundry test with competing grant/revoke signatures for same nonce; assert revocation fails after grant is front-run.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke the authorization of a liquidation callback contract (isAuthorized[liquidator][liquidateCallback]=false), causing Midnight.liquidate calls that use that callback to fail, preventing liquidation of unhealthy positions? Preconditions: liquidator has authorized a liquidate callback contract; attacker is an authorized delegate of the liquidator. Call sequence: (1) attacker signs Authorization{authorizer=liquidator, authorized=liquidateCallback, isAuthorized=false, nonce=N}; (2) attacker submits via ecrecoverAuthorizer; (3) liquidator's Midnight.liquidate(market, ..., callback=liquidateCallback, ...) reverts (callback is called after collateral transfer, so the revert happens mid-execution). Invariant tested: liquidation callback authorization must not be revocable by a delegate. Scoped impact: DoS of liquidation via callback revocation. Proof idea: unit test asserting liquidate reverts after delegate revokes callback authorization.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization with authorization.deadline == 0 (already expired), and does the check require(block.timestamp <= authorization.deadline) correctly reject it, or is there an edge case where block.timestamp == 0 (e.g., in test environments) allows it to pass? Preconditions: attacker crafts Authorization{deadline=0}. Call sequence: (1) attacker submits Authorization{deadline=0}; (2) check: block.timestamp <= 0 is false for any real block; (3) reverts Expired(). Invariant tested: expired authorizations must always revert; deadline=0 must never be valid. Scoped impact: potential bypass of deadline check in edge environments. Proof idea: unit test with deadline=0; assert revert Expired; also test with vm.warp(0) in Foundry.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant authorization to a malicious contract that, when called as an operator, reenters EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized during a Midnight callback (e.g., onBuy, onSell, onRepay) to consume additional nonces or change authorization state mid-execution? Preconditions: attacker is an authorized delegate; malicious contract is set as an operator. Call sequence: (1) attacker grants malicious contract as operator via ecrecoverAuthorizer; (2) malicious contract is called as takerCallback during Midnight.take; (3) in onBuy callback, malicious contract calls ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to revoke another operator; (4) post-callback state has unexpected authorization. Invariant tested: EcrecoverAuthorizer state changes during Midnight callbacks must not corrupt authorization invariants. Scoped impact: authorization state corruption via reentrancy. Proof idea: Foundry test with reentrant callback calling ecrecoverAuthorizer; assert authorization state is consistent post-take.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who is an authorized delegate use EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke the authorization of a withdrawCollateral operator just as the borrower needs to add more collateral to avoid liquidation, preventing the operator from calling Midnight.supplyCollateral on behalf of the borrower? Preconditions: borrower has authorized a collateral management operator; position is near liquidation; attacker is an authorized delegate. Call sequence: (1) oracle price drops; (2) attacker revokes collateral operator via ecrecoverAuthorizer; (3) operator's Midnight.supplyCollateral(market, 0, amount, borrower) reverts Unauthorized(); (4) position becomes liquidatable. Invariant tested: collateral supply authorization must not be revocable by a delegate at a critical moment. Scoped impact: forced liquidation via DoS of collateral supply. Proof idea: state test with near-liquidation position; assert supplyCollateral fails after delegate revocation.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization where the abi.encode(AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH, authorization) produces a hash collision with a different Authorization struct due to dynamic type encoding, allowing a signature for one authorization to be valid for a different one? Preconditions: attacker finds two Authorization structs with different fields that produce the same keccak256(abi.encode(AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH, authorization)). Call sequence: (1) victim signs Authorization{authorizer=A, authorized=B, isAuthorized=true, nonce=0, deadline=T}; (2) attacker constructs Authorization{authorizer=A, authorized=C, isAuthorized=true, nonce=0, deadline=T'} with same hash; (3) attacker submits with victim's signature; (4) Midnight.isAuthorized[A][C] == true. Invariant tested: EIP-712 struct hash must be collision-resistant; no two distinct Authorization structs must produce the same digest. Scoped impact: unauthorized authorization grant. Proof idea: differential test comparing abi.encode outputs for all Authorization field combinations; assert no collisions.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke the authorization of a take operator just before a maker's offer expires, preventing the operator from executing the take and causing the maker to miss the trade? Preconditions: taker has authorized an operator to execute takes; offer is about to expire; attacker is an authorized delegate of the taker. Call sequence: (1) offer.expiry == block.timestamp + 1; (2) attacker revokes take operator via ecrecoverAuthorizer; (3) operator's Midnight.take reverts TakerUnauthorized(); (4) offer expires. Invariant tested: take authorization must not be revocable by a delegate at a time-critical moment. Scoped impact: DoS of take against expiring offers. Proof idea: Foundry test with near-expiry offer; assert take fails after delegate revocation.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant themselves authorization as an operator for a victim, and then use that authorization to call Midnight.setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, victim) to cancel all of the victim's pending offers, causing DoS of all take operations? Preconditions: attacker is an authorized delegate of victim; victim has active offers. Call sequence: (1) attacker grants themselves via ecrecoverAuthorizer (already authorized as delegate); (2) attacker calls Midnight.setConsumed(offerGroup, type(uint256).max, victim); (3) all offers in group cancelled; (4) takers cannot fill victim's offers. Invariant tested: offer cancellation must require the maker's own authorization. Scoped impact: DoS of all take operations against victim's offers. Proof idea: unit test asserting setConsumed(max) cancels all offers after delegate self-grants.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization with a crafted signature where v is neither 27 nor 28, causing ecrecover to return address(0) or an unexpected address, and does the contract correctly handle all possible v values? Preconditions: attacker crafts Signature{v=0, r=..., s=...} or v=1. Call sequence: (1) attacker submits Authorization with v=0; (2) ecrecover returns address(0); (3) require(signer != address(0)) reverts InvalidSignature(). Invariant tested: all invalid v values must result in InvalidSignature revert; no unexpected address must be recovered. Scoped impact: potential signature bypass if v validation is incomplete. Proof idea: fuzz test with v in [0, 1, 2, 26, 27, 28, 29, 255]; assert all non-{27,28} values revert InvalidSignature.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke the authorization of a post-maturity liquidation operator just after market maturity, preventing the operator from calling Midnight.liquidate in postMaturityMode=true, leaving overdue debt unliquidated during the 15-minute LIF ramp window? Preconditions: market has matured; borrower has overdue debt; liquidation operator is authorized; attacker is an authorized delegate. Call sequence: (1) block.timestamp > market.maturity; (2) attacker revokes liquidation operator via ecrecoverAuthorizer; (3) operator's Midnight.liquidate(market, ..., postMaturityMode=true, ...) reverts (if operator check applies) or liquidatorGate blocks; (4) LIF ramp window passes. Invariant tested: post-maturity liquidation must remain available regardless of authorization manipulation. Scoped impact: DoS of post-maturity liquidation. Proof idea: state test with post-maturity market; assert liquidation fails after delegate revocation.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant authorization to a contract that implements IEnterGate and then use that authorization to manipulate the gate's behavior, bypassing the enter gate restriction for credit or debt increases? Preconditions: market has an enter gate; attacker is an authorized delegate of a user. Call sequence: (1) attacker grants a malicious gate-bypassing contract as operator via ecrecoverAuthorizer; (2) malicious contract calls Midnight.take on behalf of user; (3) gate check is performed against msg.sender (malicious contract), not the user; (4) gate may allow the malicious contract while blocking the user. Invariant tested: gate checks must apply to the actual beneficiary of the credit/debt increase, not the msg.sender. Scoped impact: gate bypass via authorization. Proof idea: unit test with gated market; assert gate check applies to taker, not operator.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke the authorization of a withdraw operator for a lender who has credit in a market with a reverting enter gate, preventing the lender from withdrawing their credit even though exit paths should remain available? Preconditions: market has a reverting enter gate; lender has credit; lender has authorized a withdraw operator; attacker is an authorized delegate. Call sequence: (1) enter gate starts reverting; (2) attacker revokes withdraw operator via ecrecoverAuthorizer; (3) operator's Midnight.withdraw reverts Unauthorized(); (4) lender cannot withdraw directly (no gas) and operator is revoked. Invariant tested: exit paths (withdraw) must remain available even when enter gate reverts; authorization revocation must not block exits. Scoped impact: fund freeze for lender. Proof idea: unit test with reverting gate; assert withdraw fails after delegate revocation.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant authorization to a contract that, when acting as an operator, calls Midnight.multicall to batch multiple state-changing operations (e.g., withdraw + setConsumed) in a single transaction, bypassing per-action authorization checks that would apply to individual calls? Preconditions: attacker is an authorized delegate; victim has credit and active offers. Call sequence: (1) attacker grants malicious contract as operator via ecrecoverAuthorizer; (2) malicious contract calls Midnight.multicall([withdraw(victim), setConsumed(victim, max)]); (3) victim's credit withdrawn and offers cancelled in one tx. Invariant tested: multicall must not bypass per-action authorization invariants. Scoped impact: unauthorized withdrawal and offer cancellation via multicall. Proof idea: Foundry test with multicall batching withdraw+setConsumed; assert both operations succeed with operator authorization.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke the authorization of a claim fee operator (if feeClaimer has authorized an operator), preventing the fee claimer from claiming accrued settlement or continuous fees? Preconditions: feeClaimer has authorized an operator via Midnight; attacker is an authorized delegate of feeClaimer. Call sequence: (1) attacker signs Authorization{authorizer=feeClaimer, authorized=feeClaimOperator, isAuthorized=false, nonce=N}; (2) attacker submits via ecrecoverAuthorizer; (3) feeClaimOperator's Midnight.claimSettlementFee/claimContinuousFee reverts OnlyFeeClaimer() (since feeClaimer check is msg.sender, not isAuthorized). Invariant tested: fee claim authorization must not be revocable by a delegate; fee claim must require msg.sender==feeClaimer. Scoped impact: DoS of fee claim if feeClaimer relies on an authorized operator. Proof idea: unit test asserting claimSettlementFee reverts OnlyFeeClaimer when called by operator after revocation.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke the authorization of a withdrawCollateral operator for a borrower who has debt, preventing the borrower from withdrawing collateral to repay elsewhere, and causing the position to remain locked? Preconditions: borrower has debt and collateral; borrower has authorized a collateral withdrawal operator; attacker is an authorized delegate. Call sequence: (1) attacker revokes collateral withdrawal operator via ecrecoverAuthorizer; (2) operator's Midnight.withdrawCollateral reverts Unauthorized(); (3) borrower cannot withdraw collateral to source repayment funds. Invariant tested: collateral withdrawal authorization must not be revocable by a delegate. Scoped impact: fund freeze for borrower. Proof idea: unit test asserting withdrawCollateral fails after delegate revocation.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant authorization to a contract that implements ISellCallback and, when called as a seller callback during Midnight.take, calls ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to revoke the taker's operator authorization mid-execution, causing the take to complete but leaving the taker's future operations blocked? Preconditions: attacker is an authorized delegate of taker; malicious contract is set as seller callback. Call sequence: (1) attacker grants malicious callback as operator; (2) during Midnight.take, onSell callback calls ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to revoke taker's operator; (3) take completes; (4) taker's operator cannot perform subsequent repay/withdraw. Invariant tested: authorization state changes during take callbacks must not corrupt post-take authorization invariants. Scoped impact: DoS of post-take operations. Proof idea: Foundry test with reentrant sell callback revoking operator; assert post-take repay fails.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant authorization to a contract that, when acting as an operator for a borrower, calls Midnight.repay with a malicious callback that reverts, causing the repay to fail and leaving the borrower's debt unpaid while the authorization state has been mutated? Preconditions: attacker is an authorized delegate; borrower has debt. Call sequence: (1) attacker grants malicious contract as operator via ecrecoverAuthorizer; (2) malicious contract calls Midnight.repay(market, units, borrower, maliciousCallback, data); (3) maliciousCallback.onRepay reverts; (4) repay reverts; (5) borrower's debt remains; (6) authorization state was mutated before repay attempt. Invariant tested: failed repay must not leave authorization state in a partially mutated condition. Scoped impact: DoS of repay via malicious callback. Proof idea: unit test with reverting repay callback; assert debt unchanged and authorization state consistent.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke the authorization of a take operator for a taker who is the seller in a pending offer, preventing the operator from executing the sell take and leaving the taker's collateral locked in the market? Preconditions: taker is a seller; taker has authorized an operator to execute sell takes; attacker is an authorized delegate. Call sequence: (1) attacker revokes take operator via ecrecoverAuthorizer; (2) operator's Midnight.take(sellOffer, ..., taker=seller, ...) reverts TakerUnauthorized(); (3) seller cannot exit position. Invariant tested: sell take authorization must not be revocable by a delegate. Scoped impact: fund freeze for seller. Proof idea: unit test with sell offer; assert take fails after delegate revocation.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant authorization to a contract that calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized to add a new authorized address, creating an unbounded authorization chain where each new authorized address can grant further authorizations, enabling a DoS attack by flooding the isAuthorized mapping? Preconditions: attacker is an authorized delegate; victim has authorized attacker. Call sequence: (1) attacker grants contract C1 via ecrecoverAuthorizer; (2) C1 calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized(C2, true, victim); (3) C2 calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized(C3, true, victim); (4) repeat N times; (5) victim's isAuthorized mapping has N unauthorized addresses. Invariant tested: authorization chains must not enable unbounded privilege escalation. Scoped impact: authorization state pollution and potential DoS. Proof idea: fuzz test with N-deep authorization chain; assert all N addresses can act on behalf of victim.

[File: 'src/periphery/EcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke the authorization of a liquidator gate-approved operator just before a liquidation, causing the liquidation to fail because the liquidator gate checks msg.sender (the operator) rather than the borrower, and the gate no longer approves the operator? Preconditions: market has a liquidator gate; liquidator has authorized an operator; gate approves the operator; attacker is an authorized delegate of the liquidator. Call sequence: (1) attacker revokes operator via ecrecoverAuthorizer; (2) operator's Midnight.liquidate reverts LiquidatorGatedFromLiquidating() (gate checks msg.sender==operator, not the liquidator); (3) unhealthy position remains unliquidated. Invariant tested: liquidator gate must check the actual liquidator (msg.sender), not an authorized operator; revocation must not block liquidation. Scoped impact: DoS of liquidation via gate + authorization interaction. Proof idea: unit test with gated market; assert liquidation fails after delegate revokes operator.
